posts.name: Brian Buzzini
posts.cooked:
• 2008 Louis Carillon Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru Les Perrières - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru (1/31/2018)
    This continues to be a ROCK STAR! The most beautiful shimmering gold and green color…reduction smoke, lemon/lime citrus, limestone chalk, butter sautéed green apple nose. In perfect form right now…the fruit is ripe and creamy with GC weight…pure and energized with intense citrus acidity and a wall of crushed rock minerality! Background smoky reduction, caramel green apples, honeycomb, vanilla butter, salty sea air. Endless length of flowers and spice, creamy with zip, and a funk show of geology! Never had Coche Perr(meursault)…but I’ll bet this wine would get a few good punches in, in battle! Will even get better. +pts (95 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

810×1080 195 KB

posts.name: Ryan Caughey
posts.cooked: Carillon killed it in 08. I drank mine early but glad to see they’re rockin’

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked:
I’ve had a few instances of the BBM being more advanced than the excellent Perrieres and Referts. 2005 and 2009 seem regular culprits. Of course, “regular” in this context is still a very small number of bottles but three 2005s have been quite consistently more developed than three Perrieres.
Charlie - are you saying 2008s have been a problem?

posts.name: suzanne_camhi
posts.cooked: my most recent 2007 Louis Carillon Bienvenue Batard was oxidized. Returned the opened bottle and got a store credit.

posts.name: saӏіӏ В e n e g a ӏ
posts.cooked:
B. Buzzini:
c fu:

Surprised it’s not showing advanced like so many of the bbm are

Fresh as a daisy! Looks like it’s YOU on the 08 BBM…as that’s the only negative note I see!

Had an 08 BBM just a couple of weeks ago - very good wine, but surprisingly dark/golden in colour to a point that I would have been very worried about pox if I owned any. But glad your 08 Perrieres showed so well.

That reminds me - have a 11 Perrieres in my wine fridge that would probably make for good drinking this spring.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I have also loved Carillon’s 08 Perrières. But even the Puligny villages is fantastic in 2008.

Have to wonder if advanced bottles of the BBM have to do with its higher percentage of new oak.

The Caillon ‘cellar’ (more like a basement) is pretty warm, too.

posts.name: maureen nelson
posts.cooked: The last bottle of this I opened was very reduced and shut down and an hour in the decanter barely helped. But then it was gone!

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked:
Maureen - are you saying you’d drunk it all by the time it opened, or that it went from reduced to shot in no time? And was that the Perrieres?
Compared to some of the other WBs I drink regularly, Carillon is rarely reduced. Have you had a few examples like that?

posts.name: Brad England
posts.cooked:
alan weinberg:

we will have the 2010 BBM at Don’s white Burg dinner soon.

Alan, hope you enjoy it. Carillon BBM is a favorite of our core wine group, and I think the 2010 is outstanding. Also, if you have the 2012, give it an early try as it’s also stunning for a youngster.
